people.bio:
Marriott Ogle Tarbotton (1835–1887) was one of Nottingham's most important Victorian engineers. The house at 6 South Road was built for him and his wife Emily, as established from the initials MOT and EOT on the stained glass windows — the same paintings are found at Papplewick Pumping Station, which he designed.

He started his career in Wakefield. In 1859 he was appointed Nottingham's Borough Surveyor and Engineer to the Waterworks Committee in 1880.

He carried out many important projects but is especially remembered for replacing the old bridge over the River Trent with the structure that stands today. It was widened in 1926 but passing underneath in a boat, the original portion can still be identified.

He also started the modern sewerage system, establishing Stoke Bardolph Sewerage Disposal Works. He was the first municipal Engineer in the world to employ subways under streets to carry public services — Gas, Sewers etc. When Victoria Street was created, this system was incorporated in the design.

He built the University College in Shakespeare Street, Nottingham, now part of the Trent Polytechnic, and laid the foundation for the present inner City Boulevards.

Papplewick Pumping Station stands as a fitting monument to an early municipal Engineer. He died in 1887, just a few years after he had designed Papplewick Pumping Station.
Emily Tarbotton, wife of Marriott Ogle Tarbotton. Her initials EOT appear in the stained glass windows of 6 South Road alongside those of her husband MOT.

significant_places.name: Papplewick Pumping Station
significant_places.description: Papplewick Pumping Station was designed by Marriott Ogle Tarbotton, Borough Surveyor of Nottingham, and opened in 1884. It is one of the finest examples of Victorian engineering in England, built to supply clean water to Nottingham. The initials MOT (Marriott Ogle Tarbotton) appear in the stained glass windows of the pumping station, matching those found in the windows of his home at 6 South Road.

person_connections.connection: Designer of Papplewick Pumping Station; his initials MOT appear in its stained glass windows

significant_places.name: Trent Bridge, Nottingham
significant_places.description: The current Trent Bridge was designed by Marriott Ogle Tarbotton, Borough Surveyor of Nottingham, replacing the old bridge over the River Trent. The original structure, widened in 1926, can still be identified from beneath.

significant_places.name: Stoke Bardolph Sewage Works
significant_places.description: Stoke Bardolph Sewerage Disposal Works was established by Marriott Ogle Tarbotton as part of Nottingham's modern sewerage system.

person_connections.connection: Established Stoke Bardolph Sewerage Disposal Works as part of Nottingham's modern sewerage system
